narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 58.9% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which is right around the average for students in our dataset, your LSAT score of 30, your lower-middle family income bracket, and your ranks in the bottom deciles for both your first-year and third-year grades at school—specifically, a second decile in Year 1 and the very bottom first decile in Year 3. You're also a Black male planning to attend full-time, which aligns with common patterns in the data where males make up about 56% and full-time students 93%, while Black students represent just under 5%. When the model weighed all this, your third-year grade decile had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure because it was so much lower than the typical fifth-decile average. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which also nudged the odds toward failure compared to the dataset's average of nearly 37. Your first-year decile rank came next in influence, similarly contributing to the concern due to its below-average standing. Factors like your family income and racial background followed closely, each adding a bit more weight toward the failure prediction in your case. Your undergraduate GPA had a milder but still concerning push in the same direction, while your gender as male actually helped a little by slightly favoring a passing prediction. Being full-time was a minor factor leaning toward risk but not a big driver. Overall, these academic performance indicators—especially your later-year grades and LSAT—stood out as the top concerns because they deviated most sharply from what succeeds for typical students who pass the bar first time. To improve your future chances, focus on building stronger study habits and academic consistency, perhaps by retaking the LSAT to boost that score toward the average or higher, as that's something you can realistically target. Addressing any gaps shown in your school deciles through targeted prep courses could also signal better bar readiness down the line. We encourage you to use this feedback as a roadmap—many students turn things around with focused effort.
